content:
The patient presented to the emergency department with acute onset substernal chest pain and dyspnea. Initial electrocardiogram demonstrated ST-segment depression in the anterior leads with reciprocal changes. Troponin I was elevated at 2.8 ng/mL, consistent with acute myocardial infarction. Coronary angiography revealed a critical stenosis in the mid-left anterior descending artery with TIMI 2 flow. The patient underwent successful percutaneous coronary intervention with placement of a drug-eluting stent to the LAD with restoration of TIMI 3 flow. Transthoracic echocardiography performed on hospital day two showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 38% with anterior wall hypokinesis and mild mitral regurgitation. B-type natriuretic peptide was mildly elevated at 285 pg/mL.

During the hospital course, the patient was initiated on dual antiplatelet therapy with aspirin and ticagrelor, along with high-intensity statin therapy. An ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker were uptitrated as tolerated for cardioprotection and heart failure management. The patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout the admission without recurrent chest pain or arrhythmia. Cardiac rehabilitation was consulted and the patient participated in early mobilization with good tolerance. Repeat troponin levels trended appropriately downward, and the patient was discharged on hospital day seven in stable condition.

The patient is being discharged on optimal medical therapy with close outpatient cardiology follow-up scheduled within two weeks. Repeat transthoracic echocardiography is planned in six weeks to reassess left ventricular function and guide further therapeutic decisions. The patient has been counseled on medication adherence, dietary sodium restriction, and participation in phase II cardiac rehabilitation. He was advised to seek immediate medical attention for recurrent chest pain, shortness of breath, or syncope.
